sources: Avoma discovery call 2026-06-19, Caroline Lacocque (Zycus): 'Zycus modular APIs can integrate with SAP/Ariba,' confirming Ariba as incumbent.
Account context: Tata Steel Nederland BV is a large European steel manufacturer, industry and geography strongly suggest SAP ERP and SAP Ariba as incumbent S2P platform.
Avoma discovery call 2026-06-19, Lucy Li: requested AI use-case materials, signaling AI capability as evaluation priority and potential wedge against incumbent.
competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the incumbent S2P platform, confirmed on the 2026-06-19 discovery call when Caroline stated 'Zycus modular APIs can integrate with SAP/Ariba' in response to the integration discussion. Tata Steel Nederland (large European steel manufacturer, likely SAP ERP shop) almost certainly runs SAP Ariba as the current procurement suite, making SAP the primary competitor defending an entrenched position. Integration complexity (connectors, data migration, SSO, user provisioning), user adoption (retraining, workflow migration), and SAP's aggressive defense of the install base are the competitive hurdles Zycus must overcome. Caroline positioned Zycus's modular integration capability and Merlin AI (agentic procurement, Teams intake agent) as the offset to switching cost, and Lucy's focus on 'AI-driven transformation' and requests for AI use-case materials (Heineken, Tata Play) signal that AI maturity is the wedge Zycus can win on. However, no competitive positioning discussion, SAP Ariba feature gap analysis, or buyer dissatisfaction with the incumbent has been documented yet. The prior RFP (cancelled March 2026 due to budget constraints) may have included SAP Ariba as a participant or may have been an Ariba renewal that was deferred, but this is unknown. No other competitor (Coupa, GEP, Ivalua, Jaggaer) has been mentioned. How we win: position Merlin AI and agentic procurement as a generational leap over Ariba's static workflows, demonstrate ROI that offsets switching cost, and provide seamless SAP/Ariba integration connectors to de-risk the migration. Next move: during the July demo, ask Lucy what she likes and dislikes about the current system (Ariba), probe for feature gaps and pain points with the incumbent, and position Merlin AI use cases as the differentiation.

identify_pain.narrative: On the 2026-06-19 discovery call, Lucy Li stated that the 'previous RFP with Tata was halted in March due to budget constraints' and that this new engagement is focused on 'procurement excellence and AI-driven transformation.' She requested AI use-case materials, including 'Heineken AI use-case demo,' 'Tata Play agentic AI use-case materials,' and 'short AI use-case write-ups for procurement excellence internal review,' signaling that the pain is procurement process inefficiency and the desire to modernize with AI-driven automation (intake, sourcing, contracting). Caroline positioned 'Merlin Teams intake agent' as relevant to user adoption and process transformation, and the broad product scope (9 modules: iContract, iSource, iManage, eProcurement, iRequest, iSaaS, Merlin, iLogix, Merlin Intake) confirms pain across the full procurement lifecycle. However, no specific operational pain (e.g. slow sourcing cycle time, manual intake bottlenecks, supplier risk, contract compliance gaps, spend visibility) or financial pain (e.g. cost leakage, FTE burden, savings leakage) was articulated with specifics or quantified. The pain is real (evidenced by the prior RFP attempt and the 9-module scope) but is described qualitatively as 'procurement excellence and AI transformation,' not operationally or financially. The pain owner is Lucy Li (Procurement Process Improvement Lead) and likely her leadership (CPO Elfranco Van Loggerenberg and the procurement excellence team she mentioned), but the CPO has not yet validated the pain or prioritized it. Next move: during the July demo, probe for specific pain points (what breaks today, where time/cost is lost, what procurement excellence KPIs are tracked) and quantify the impact to build the business case.
